steps.title: Locate the subject and the existing finite verb
steps.content: The relative clause is "whose work ______ the richness of Betham's poems and the poet's historical importance to the Romantic literary movement encourages a broader rethinking of British Romanticism itself." The subject is "whose work," and the clause already has a finite main verb later in the sentence: "encourages." The blank cannot add a second finite verb without a coordinator, or the clause becomes a run-on.

steps.title: Identify the grammar issue and apply the rule
steps.content:
Identify the grammar issue: post-nominal participial phrase vs. extra finite verb. Rule: a single clause can have only one finite verb per subject unless the verbs are joined by a coordinating conjunction. Since "encourages" is already the finite verb governing "whose work," the blank must be a non-finite modifier (a participial phrase) describing "work," not a second finite verb.
  • Choice A — ‘has highlighted’: finite present-perfect verb. Pairing it with the later finite verb "encourages" gives "whose work has highlighted X and Y encourages Z" — two finite verbs sharing one subject with no conjunction. Run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘is highlighting’: finite present-progressive verb. Same defect as A — it competes with "encourages" for the role of main verb in the relative clause and yields a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘highlighting’: non-finite present participle. It introduces a participial phrase modifying "work" ("whose work, highlighting the richness... and the poet's historical importance..., encourages a broader rethinking..."), leaving "encourages" as the single finite verb of the relative clause. Grammatical. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘highlights’: finite third-person-singular verb. Although it agrees with "work," it produces the same run-on as A and B: "whose work highlights X and Y encourages Z" has two finite verbs for one subject with no coordinator. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
